items.title: Could First Nations wisdom and experience improve mental health and wellbeing?
items.summary: The latest report on Closing the Gap Targets, released last month, found just four of the nineteen targets are on track. Among those targets Australia is failing to meet, the country is moving backward when it comes to reducing suicide rates for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people , which are now more than three times higher than for non-Indigenous Australians. First Nations health advocates and practitioners are urgently calling for investment in new approaches to preventative mental health led by First Nations wisdom and experience.

items.title: Victorian and Tasmanian teachers go on strike
items.summary: Tens of thousands of education workers have walked off the job and marched on the Victorian parliament over a pay dispute. It's the first time in 13 years the state's teachers, principals and support staff have staged a 24-hour stoppage, severely disrupting lessons in hundreds of schools.

items.title: Australia signs $10 billion trade and security pact with the European Union
items.summary: Australia and the European Union have signed a landmark $10 billion trade and security pact to eliminate industrial tariffs and strengthen co-operation on cyber and maritime defence. While the Prime Minister has hailed it as an economic win, the deal has faced criticism from the National Party regarding agricultural access, and from the Greens over Australia's strategic dependency on Europe.

items.title: How the war in the Middle East is triggering concerns over food security
items.summary: The war in the Middle East, and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, has triggered what experts describe as one of the most significant energy crises in decades. But oil and gas are not the only vital resources that are impacted. The war has also severely disrupted the flow of fertiliser from the Middle East, triggering concerns over the impacts on local food production and food security.

items.title: INTERVIEW: How much firepower does Iran have left?
items.summary: The recent attempt by Iran to launch a missile attack on the UK-US air base on the island of Diego Garcia in the middle of the Indian Ocean has raised questions about the real strength of Iran's firepower. Iran previously has put a self-imposed limit on its ballistic missile program, limiting their range to (2,000 kilometers. Diego Garcia is well outside that range, 4000 kilometres from Iran. And as the war drags on, how much weaponry does Iran have in reserve? Justin Bronk, an airpower and defence analyst at defence think tank the Royal United Services Institute, has told the Associated Press Iran's resources are not limitless.

items.title: Rhoda Roberts AO: The cultural warrior who changed the nation
items.summary: Tributes are being made to SBS Elder-in-residence and Widjabul Wia-bal woman, Auntie Rhoda Roberts, who has died at the age of 66. Her short battle with a very rare type of ovarian cancer ended on Saturday afternoon on her country surrounded by family. The decorated writer, actor and director was awarded an Order of Australia in 2016 for her service to performing arts and immense impact on and off the stage and screen. Rhoda Robert’s family has granted permission for SBS to broadcast images and audio, to honour and celebrate her life and enduring legacy.

items.title: INTERVIEW: Ahead of South Australia's state election, SBS talks to One Nation candidate Cory Bernardi
items.summary: South Australians head to the polls on Saturday, 21 March, to elect the state’s 56th Parliament, with the latest YouGov poll pointing to a strong result for Labor. The survey suggests Labor could achieve its highest-ever two-party preferred vote in South Australia, while the Liberal Party is tracking toward a historically poor outcome. Meanwhile, Pauline Hanson’s One Nation is polling at 22 per cent, signalling a significant presence in the vote. SBS Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson spoke with One Nation candidate and former Liberal Senator Cory Bernardi.

items.summary: South Australia votes to elect its 56th Parliament on Saturday, 21 March. YouGov’s final poll ahead of the election showed Labor is on track to secure its highest-ever two-party preferred vote in the state party's history, while the Liberals face their worst result to date. One Nation however is at 22 percent. SBS Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson has spoken with South Australian Premier Peter Malinauskas about his hopes for the election - and what he thinks of Pauline Hanson.

items.title: Scottish MPs reject assisted dying - and England's House of Lords looks set to follow suit
items.summary: A WARNING THAT THIS STORY MAY BE DISTRESSING FOR SOME LISTENERS: Scotland's Parliament has rejected legislation that would have made Scotland the first part of the United Kingdom to allow terminally ill adults to end their lives. Members of the Edinburgh-based legislature voted 69 to 57 against a bill that would have let people in Scotland with six months or less to live seek help to end their life. The Scottish vote is part of a wider debate across the UK.

items.title: How do Australia's petrol prices compare globally?
items.summary: Australia's petrol prices have surged in recent weeks, but remain lower than in many parts of the world.

items.title: I was on reality tv and lead a high-flying lifestyle. People envy me — I don't blame them
items.summary: My life is centred around luxury — and I essentially drink for a living, writes former Real Housewives star Kyla Kirkpatrick.

items.title: New mining CEOs, plus markets brace for US rates decision
items.summary: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Jamie Hannah from VanEck about the day's market action including the latest global interest rate expectations and what it means for investments, while BHP announces who will replace CEO Mike Henry.

items.summary: Trump's latest outburst exposes the tightrope American allies are balancing on amid the war in the Middle East.

items.title: Four-day week, jackets off: The world starts cutting back on fuel amid supply concerns
items.summary: As parts of Asia adopt fuel rationing measures, the Australian government insists it's not on the cards.

items.title: SBS News In Easy English 18 March 2026
items.summary: A daily 5 minute news bulletin for English learners and people with a disability.

items.title: Zelenskyy, Starmer urge continuing support for Ukraine as world's attention turns to Iran
items.summary: The war in Ukraine is intersecting with rising instability in the Middle East and pressure on global energy supplies. President Volodymyr Zelenskyy is in London, warning modern warfare is rapidly evolving and pushing for deeper defence cooperation. At the same time, the EU is moving to resolve an oil dispute threatening support for Kyiv.

items.title: UN rights office says Israeli strikes on Lebanese civilians may be war crimes
items.summary: The UN human rights office has called for an investigation into deadly strikes on displaced people sheltering in tents.

items.title: Trump says he doesn't 'need or desire' Australia's help in outburst over Strait of Hormuz
items.summary: Trump said his NATO allies were making a "very foolish mistake" and lumped Australia in on the list of countries he's unhappy with.

items.title: 'Cashing in big time': Who's profiting from the war in the Middle East?
items.summary: War in the Middle East has left hundreds of people dead and left destruction in its wake — and there's also an "uncomfortable truth".

items.title: The 90-day question looming over Australia's fuel price pain
items.summary: Global oil disruption has put Australia's fuel security back in focus.

items.title: US claims oil tankers beginning to cross Strait of Hormuz
items.summary: White House economic adviser Kevin Hassett called the movement "a sign of how little Iran has left."

items.title: RBA raises interest rates in March for second time in 2026
items.summary: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Nick Stenner from Bank of America; Alice Shen from VanEck; and Sally Tindall from Canstar to find out why the RBA lifted interest rates, how the market reacted and what it means for borrowers.

items.title: Matildas to take on nine-time Asian Cup winners China, with a familiar face on the team
items.summary: Matildas head coach Joe Montemurro says tonight's game will be a "tactical battle".

items.title: Afghanistan says 400 killed in Kabul hospital attack as Pakistan denies responsibility
items.summary: Afghan authorities allege a drug-user rehabilitation hospital was hit amid an escalating conflict with its neighbour, Pakistan.

items.title: Interest rates go up: decision reflects growing uncertainty in the global economy
items.summary: The Reserve Bank has hiked interest rates by 25 basis points to 4.1 per cent. It's the second rise in a row - and there could be more to come.

items.title: Despite a rate rise, Australia's CPI will likely remain among highest in the world
items.summary: Australia's consumer price index of 3.8 per cent is one of the highest in the world.

items.title: Has Vienna solved the problem on housing affordability?
items.summary: Rental affordability has hit a new low in Australia - but a European city boasts a century-old social housing model, which houses about 50 per cent of its population. With low rental costs and rolling leases, it's led some to refer to it as a 'renter's utopia'.

items.title: Australians in Lebanon face $17k travel nightmare as daughter needs medical care
items.summary: The family had planned to come back to Australia at the end of April, but are now trying to return more urgently.

items.title: Taiwanese Indigenous dance at Women's Asian Cup briefly cancelled, politician claims
items.summary: A Taiwanese Indigenous dance performance was reinstated before the Taiwan-China football quarter final on Saturday, after diplomats intervened.

items.title: 'We don't want to lose our town': Woolworths' move strikes fear among residents
items.summary: The closure of Rio Tinto's bauxite mine will be tough enough for this Arnhem Land town, but now Woolworths may pull out too.

items.title: Allies resist President Trump's calls to send warships to Strait of Hormuz
items.summary: Global oil prices have increased by 40 to 50 percent as Iran attacks shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. President Donald Trump says the United States is negotiating with countries heavily reliant on Middle East oil, after he demanded several nations send warships to re-open the Strait of Hormuz. But European allies have so far proved resistant to President Trump's request.

items.title: Australia not among US allies asked to send navy vessels to the Strait of Hormuz
items.summary: Defence Minister Richard Marles says the government would consider any request to participate in the conflict, but none had yet arrived.

items.title: Israeli ground offensive in Lebanon 'must be averted', countries say in joint statement
items.summary: Leaders of Canada, France, Germany, Italy and the UK have warned of "devastating consequences" as Israel says it's conducting a ground operation.

items.title: The RBA faces its 'most complicated' rates call in years. What could it mean for you?
items.summary: The Reserve Bank was until recently expected to keep rates on hold at today's meeting. Now economists aren't so sure.

items.title: Justin says he was a jealous boyfriend. Then he found a 'long-term fix'
items.summary: "Constant reassurance" was a short-term fix for Justin's jealousy. It took therapy, time and meditation to find the "long-term" one.

items.title: INTERVIEW: Race Discrimination Commissioner Giridharan Sivaraman
items.summary: In a week that began with the International Day to Combat Islamophobia, SBS's Tee Mitchell spoke to Australia's Race Discrimination Commissioner at the Human Rights Commission, Giridharan Sivaraman, about Islamophobia in this country and race relations in general.

items.title: Government defends decision on Iranian women’s football team visas
Markets brace for rate rise and can you trust AI for financial advice?
items.summary: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Mathan Somasundaram from Deep Data Analytics and looks ahead to tomorrow's RBA interest rate decision on what will be a big week for global central banks; plus Stephanie Youssef looks at how Generation Z is seeking out financial advice with ASIC's Adam Kirkland.

items.title: 'One Battle After Another' also wins Oscars battle
items.summary: One Battle After Another has bested Sinners at this year's Oscars, winning six awards to Sinners' four. KPop Demon Hunters and Frankenstein were among the other winners at Hollywood's night of nights. Michael B. Jordan and Jessie Buckley won the top acting gongs.

items.title: Does a 'Great Firewall' loom? Why Russians are buying up walkie-talkies and road maps
items.summary: Some believe Russia is testing a system that could cut it off from the global internet.

items.title: INTERVIEW: Will the war in the Middle East cause the RBA to raise interest rates?
items.summary: The war in the Middle East has rocked the financial world, with the price of oil sky rocketing and no end to the conflict in sight. This week will see the Reserve Bank set the interest rate - and the markets are now predicting there's likely to be a hike in the rates. Cameron Carr spoke to the AMP's Chief Economist Shane Oliver. He says it's an uncertain situation.

items.title: Questions raised over Australia's long-term energy security
items.summary: The Federal Government has authorised a temporary 60-day easing of sulphur standards to inject high-sulphur petrol into regional communities facing critical fuel shortages. While Energy Minister Chris Bowen maintains that national reserves are stable, critics and analysts warn that Australia’s decade-long failure to meet international stockpile obligations leaves the country vulnerable to prolonged geopolitical shocks.

items.title: How to make SBS News your preferred source on Google
items.summary: Google now allows you to pick your preferred sources in Top Stories.

items.title: Fresh warning over risky financial advice targeting young Australians
items.summary: A survey commissioned by the country's financial watchdog suggests nearly two thirds of gen Z trust financial information on social media.

items.title: Sentimental Value just won an Oscar. Meet the skateboard-smuggling director behind it
items.summary: Joachim Trier reflects on his family, punk roots, messy lives and the quiet moments that inspire his films.

items.title: Recession not expected in Australia but markets suggest a March rate hike: Treasurer
items.summary: Traders and economists widely expect the Reserve Bank to lift interest rates but uncertainty over the Iran war could convince the board to keep them on hold.

items.title: How is watching the war in the Middle East impacting our mental health?
items.summary: The recent conflict in Iran and across the Middle East is exposing audiences to atrocities and human suffering on a large scale. Here's how people can stay informed, without becoming overwhelmingly distressed.

items.title: Frozen in time: the legacy of the people of Pompeii
items.summary: A new permanent exhibition on the ancient Roman city of Pompeii has showcased an arresting image of the moment when a catastrophic volcano eruption destroyed the city in 79 AD. Over 20 casts of bodies of residents in the city will be displayed at the exhibition, along with other findings such as plants and food that remained buried for centuries under metres of ash and lava.

items.title: Fifth member of Iranian football team leaves Australia after withdrawing asylum bid
items.summary: Team captain Zahra Ghanbari has withdrawn her asylum bid, after three other members of the team left Australia over the weekend.

items.title: For 200 years, this community has called an Australian island home. Now its future is in doubt
items.summary: In one of Australia's most remote places, a unique community navigates rising seas and fears of relocation and cultural loss.

items.title: Max lives in a 'renters' utopia', but some say the city's famous housing system isn't fair
items.summary: As rental affordability hits a new low in Australia, a European city boasts a decades-long social housing model which houses about 50 per cent of its population.

items.title: Punters 'unlikely' to see millions in ticket sales refunded as long-running festival shutters
items.summary: Bluesfest's founder said it was "unlikely" festivalgoers, some of whom spent thousands on tickets and accommodation, would see any refunds.

items.title: Energy minister urges calm over fuel stocks as consumer watchdog scrutinises price increases
items.summary: The government has assured Australians that there is no fuel supply shortage, as the New South Wales government convenes a fuel security roundtable.

items.title: Eleven dead and more than 20 injured in Sudan drone strike
items.summary: Drones have become a key ‌weapon in the war between Sudan's army and the Rapid Support Forces that began in April 2023.

items.title: Popular tax discount on electric vehicles could be phased out, just as petrol prices rise
items.summary: Proponents fear ending the Electric Car Discount could unfairly punish families outside major metropolitan areas battling long commutes.

items.title: 'A piece of living history': How Greek milk bars changed the way Australians meet and eat
items.summary: Australia's historic milk bars, rooted in Greek-Australian culture, keep nostalgia alive with classic treats and timeless charm.

items.title: Father and son counter religious hatred with 'Cake not Hate' campaign
items.summary: Across the UK, hate crimes targeting Muslims have become increasingly prevalent, and are now at record levels, representing almost half of all religious hate crimes in the country. At a local mosque in Peterborough, community members have responded by celebrating a campaign featuring kindness and cupcakes.

items.title: How the humble milk bar forever changed Australia
items.summary: Milk bars, often run by Greek migrants, forever changed the way Australians meet and eat. Across the country, cafes and milk bars popularised juke boxes, rock and roll and frothy soda drinks.

items.title: Amid rationing and shortage fears, here's what Australia's fuel supply looks like in charts
items.summary: Energy Minister Chris Bowen has released 20 per cent of the nation's fuel reserves as experts warn rationing could be on the cards.

items.title: How war in the Middle East is 're-ordering power' and what it means for Australia's alliances
items.summary: The war in the Middle East has signalled changes to the international order, with some saying the system is in "severe crisis".

items.title: Wyatt Flores and country music as therapy for mental health
items.summary: The US country star sees music as an invaluable way to spark conversations about mental health — and give people hope.

items.title: $200bn wiped off ASX since start of Middle-East war ahead of RBA meeting
items.summary: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Morgans Chief Economist Michael Knox who is betting the RBA will need to lift interest rates three times this year as a wave of inflation prepares to come down on the economy; while Stephanie Youssef looks through the day's market action with Zoran Kresovic from Blueberry and how investors are feeling as the Middle-East war rolls on.

items.title: Lebanon at 'breaking point', UN warns, as displacement crisis grows
items.summary: Lebanon's civilians bear the brunt as fighting spreads, triggering mass displacement and growing international concern.

items.title: Another conflict is dominating headlines. But what impact does the grim news cycle have on our mental health?
items.summary: The recent conflict in Iran and across the Middle East is yet again exposing news audiences to atrocities and human suffering on a large scale. So, what impact does news consumption have on audiences, and how can people stay informed, without becoming distressed?

items.title: Iran's Supreme Leader vows revenge as Israel steps up strikes
items.summary: Iran’s new Supreme Leader Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei has vowed retaliation for attacks on Iran as Israeli strikes expand, raising fears the Middle East conflict could widen and further destabilise global energy markets

items.title: 'Only one winner in this war': How the war on Iran is bankrolling the war on Ukraine
items.summary: New data shows that Russia's oil export earnings have soared since the US-Israeli war in Iran broke out, sparking fears that Russia will use this war to finance its own in Ukraine. As the United States lifts sanctions and Europe considers more flexibility, Russia says the world is coming to understand the importance of its resources.

items.title: Australian officials pulled from Lebanon, Israel and UAE amid war in the Middle East
items.summary: Non-essential government staff were told to get out of Israel and the UAE earlier on Thursday.

items.title: Buying a unit is cheaper than renting in this inner-city spot
items.summary: New research suggests Australians could save hundreds per fortnight by buying rather than renting a unit in certain places.

items.title: First people arrested under new Queensland laws banning 'from the river to the sea' chant
items.summary: The Queensland government is under fire from activists who consider the hate speech laws to be "hallmarks of an authoritarian police state".

items.title: AI claims 1,600 jobs at Atlassian & more calls for 2 RBA rate rises
items.summary: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Tom Gilbertson from Loftus Peak about the future of AI and the workforce and what it means for investors as Atlassian becomes the first major Software as a Service to slash jobs; Trent Sauders from CBA details why the bank is now predicting the RBA will lift interest rates two more times this cycle; and Angus Geddes from Fat Prophets shares his thoughts on the investment environment as the war in the Middle-East continues.

items.title: Their journey has just 'begun': Concerns Iranian players monitored by regime 'agents'
items.summary: Some are worried about the safety of Iranian players who sought asylum in Australia, raising concerns Iran could keep a close eye on them.

items.title: Oil price spikes, Trump’s Iran u-turns and: are you ready player one?
items.summary: Trump tries to extinguish the oil price fire but Iran and Israel both double down on an extended war. Iranian women soccer players seek asylum and the Nepali youth’s hip-hop hero. Plus, Ready Player One? The virtual world to the rescue…

items.title: Soldiers deployed in Johannesburg as gang violence and illegal mining grips South African cities
items.summary: Soldiers have been deployed to the streets of Johannesburg in the first major troop deployment since South African President Cyril Ramaphosa vowed to crack down on violent crime and illegal mining. With some of the highest crime rates in the world, South Africa is deploying troops to five of its nine provinces in an effort to tackle the growing violence.

items.title: I'm a scientist. One conversation led me to rethink climate change
items.summary: As a teenager growing up in a rural town, Jacob Martin was sceptical about climate change. Some pivotal moments in his life changed that.

items.title: Before offering refuge to Iranian players, Australia took in another team
items.summary: Several members of the Iranian football team have chosen asylum in Australia. But it isn't the first time the country has stepped in.

items.title: Criticism of Australia's new laws to temporarily ban some people coming into the country
items.summary: The federal government has introduced new legislation to impose a temporary ban from some countries travelling to Australia on certain visas. Rights groups and experts have condemned the move, raising concerns that the war in the Middle East could prevent people from seeking asylum in Australia.

items.title: Oil price threatens taxi fare increases and major banks now predict two more rate rises
items.summary: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Josh Gilbert from eToro to as NAB, Westpac and Citi predict two RBA interest rate rises in a row as oil prices remain high threatening inflation; and Stephanie Youssef speaks with Blair Davies from the Australian Taxi Industry Association to find out, if like some airlines, taxis will need to increase fares to cover the spike in petrol prices.

items.title: 'Hypocritical': Government moves to curb some Iranian visas after footballer asylum cases
items.summary: Critics accuse the government of concealing the "true effect" of the changes, arguing they would prevent people from seeking refuge.

items.title: 'I think our country needs to be saved': Nationals elect Matt Canavan as new leader
items.summary: Queensland Senator Matt Canavan has been elected leader of the Nationals following David Littleproud's resignation, appointing Darren Chester as his deputy. The conservative leader aims to challenge net-zero targets, address fuel crises, and advocate for child care reform.

items.title: How new superannuation changes will impact you
items.summary: The government's much-debated changes to superannuation have now passed the Senate, and are expected to become law by the end of the week.
